titleStmt.title: Comparison between the effect of ac vated waters on len l seed germina on using various plasma reactors and hydrogen injec on system

affiliation.orgName: Iran University of Science and Technology
Iran University of Science and Technology
Laser and plasma research ins tute

analytic.title: Comparison between the effect of ac vated waters on len l seed germina on using various plasma reactors and hydrogen injec on system

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: As a threat to mee ng the global demand for food created by the con nued growth of the popula on, different methods are being applied for enhancing seed germina on and plant growth. This study inves gates the effect of hydrogen-riched water (HRW), plasma-ac vated water (PAW) and their combina on on the seed germina on of len ls. For ac va ng water, arc discharge reactors are generated under atmospheric pressure in the air. Simula ons were also performed to simulate electric field and poten al, fluid flow, and arc plasma. Using op cal emission spectroscopy, species were evaluated in plasma columns. Raman spectra and physicochemical proper es of water were inves gated. On day 3 a er treatment, the frac on and length of germinated seeds were evaluated. During germina on, treated water significantly increased germina on parameters such as final germina on percentage, mean germina on me, germina on index, coefficient of germina on velocity and germina on rate index. It can, therefore, be concluded that seed germina on can be increased using PAW and hydrogenated PAW combined.

div.head: I. Introduc on
div.p: A threat to mee ng global demand for food has been created by the con nued growth of the popula on all over the world. Various techniques, such as different fer lizers, pes cides, water treatment, heat disinfec on, etc., are being applied to increase crop yields [1][2][3][4] . However, these methods also have a certain number of problems which include adverse impacts on health crops, eventuate pollu on a er the treatments and adding toxicity to produce various disease challenges for animals and humans as well as economic impossibility etc. 2,[5][6][7] As we strive towards finding new ways of promo ng sustainable and economically viable solu ons for enhancing seed germina on and plant growth, one op on we are currently exploring is plasma ac vated water (PAW). PAW is created by transferring various reac ve oxygen-nitrogen species (RONS) from the plasma phase to water. This process results in the forma on of a range of primary species such as atomic oxygen, single oxygen, superoxide, ozone, hydroxyl radicals, and atomic nitrogen, which are then followed by secondary species such as hydrogen peroxide, peroxy-nitrite, nitric oxide, nitrates, and nitrite ions. These secondary species react with water to form PAW, poten ally promo ng healthy plant growth 8,9 . The dissolu on of plasma species in water causes the forma on of short-lived species (O, • OH, ONOOH, and NO, etc.) and long-lived species (NO3‾, NO2‾, H2O2, and dissolved O3, etc.) 10 . The existence of these reac ve radicals and species in PAW makes it a propi ous product that can be employed in countless applica ons, including elimina ng or reducing the spread of pathogens 11 , improving soil fer lity 12 , seed germina on and plant growth 9 , etc.

PAW has been shown to affect seed germina on and plant growth with prior reports from various researchers 9,13,14 . Rathore et al. 13 water ac vated with a pencil plasma jet (PPJ) improves the germina on rate, viability index, and mean germina on me of peas (Pisum sa vum L.). Sivachandiran et al. 9 have reported the posi ve effect of air plasma and PAW with a DBD reactor on germina on rate and plant growth in 3 different seed species, including radish, tomatoes and sweet pepper. Terebun et al. 14 describes the significant improvement of PAW produced in an atmospheric pressure gliding arc reactor for the germina on of beetroot (Beta vulgaris) and carrot (Daucus carota) seeds.

Addi onally, a series of events in the growth and development of plants have been a ributed to H2 15,16 . The role and basic mechanisms for H2 in promo ng seed germina on were inves gated by some researchers, such as Huang et al. 17 . In recent years, H2 is an en rely new an oxidant in animals and plants. As a new beneficial gaseous molecule, H2 can respond to physiological processes [18][19][20] . In addi on, H2 responds to certain abio c stress factors such as salinity 21 , osmo c stresses 22 , heavy metals 23 , high light stress 24 and temperatures 25 . For instance, an increase in the an oxidant system that counteracts the overproduc on of ROS and lipid peroxida on has led to increased salt tolerance of Arabidopsis through Hydrogen-Rich Water (HRW) 21 .

Furthermore, the cul va on of food legumes, which provides more than 1 billion people with a key source of diges ble carbohydrate polymers, could provide opportuni es for use in new plant protein-based foods and animal feeding stuff.

Seeds of grain legumes are very popular as a source of diges ble carbohydrates, compared to animal-based protein, which is environmentally costly. The value of food, handling characteris cs and gustatory quali es can be provided by carbohydrate polymers 26 . The ancient crop, which is produced in more than 70 countries, is Len ls (Lens culinaris Medik.) 27 . The seeds come in a variety of colors and pa erns and have a lens-like shape 28 . Large green len ls are sold mainly in European markets and parts of the Middle East and South America 29 . Len l seeds offer a significant source of dietary fiber, complex carbohydrates, iron, zinc, and vitamin B complex. These nutri onal components are essen al for maintaining a healthy diet and providing the body with the necessary nutrients for op mal performance 30 . They also contain specific phenolic compounds, which give them a high an oxidant ac vity compared to other legume species 31 .

Different methods of spectroscopy such as op cal emission and Raman spectroscopy can provide important informa on on chemical agents. Raman spectroscopy is an efficient way of obtaining chemical informa on by means of the distribu on of electromagne c radia on through molecular vibra ons in ma er 32 . Due to the reduc on of or elimina on of limita ons on Raman Spectroscopy, it is beginning to be applied more and more for qualita ve analysis 33- 35 .

In previous work, finite element simula on inves gated two-dimensional dielectric barrier discharge and arc discharge in a bubble inside water 36 . The present study uses an HRW generator and two different atmospheric pressure air plasma generators to produce PAW.

In order to gain a be er understanding of the electrical and fluidic parameters of plasma, simula on was performed to explore electric fields and poten als as well as fluid flow and plasma arcs. Species were evaluated in plasma columns by op cal emission spectroscopy and Raman spectra of water were inves gated. Mul farious produced waters were exposed to green Len ls (Lens culinaris Medik.) seeds to study the various phenotypic, growth parameters, etc.

div.head: II. Material and method A. Setups
div.p: The schematic of the water activating setups illustrated in FIG. 1. An output power of 15 kV at 55 kHz frequency and a maximum intensity of 130W is provided by the high voltage power supply used for plasma generation. In this research, PAW production systems are based on arc discharge plasma. A glass tube, an air pump, a rubber pipe and two cylindrical electrodes with a diameter of 1 mm shall be part of the 1st system. The discharge shall take place between the electrodes in a glass tube connected to an air pump. Through a plastic tube, the species formed in the plasma are injected into the water (FIG. 1. a). A needle electrode with a shape of 0.7 mm, placed at a height of approximately 3.5 cm from the water surface, and a flat electrode placed in the water, make the other configuration. In this system, the plasma plume is formed between the needle electrode and the water and is always in contact with the water (FIG.

div.head: C. Prepara on of len l seeds
div.p: Green dried len l seeds (L. culinaris) were purchased from the local market. A total number of 750 seeds (6 groups'× 5 replicate× 25 seeds) were divided into 6 groups named control, PAW1, PAW2, HPAW1, HPAW2, and HRW. Each group was further subdivided into 5 sets of 25 seeds. Each set (25 seeds) of the group soaked in 7 ml of sample (Control, PAW1, PAW2, HPAW1, HPAW2, and HRW) for 72 h (24 °C, 26% Rela ve humidity).

div.head: D. Simula on
div.p: The Electrosta c Module within Comsol Mul physics which enables the simula on of electric fields and charged par cles and understanding the electrical parameters of plasma, such as electric poten al distribu on, electric field strength, and charge density as well as, the Laminar Flow Module which simulates the flow of fluid within the plasma reactor are used in this study. It considers the laminar flow regime, where fluid par cles move in smooth layers or streamlines without significant mixing. This module helps to inves gate plasma fluidic parameters, such as velocity profiles, pressure distribu on, and flow pa erns.

div.head: FIG. 2. Schema c of produc on of various water u lized in this study
div.p: Two different 2D geometries were designed for two plasma reactors, as illustrated in Figure 3. This figure illustrates the indirect arc reactor in FIG. 1.a and a direct arc reactor in FIG. 1.b. For simula ng the electric field inside a plasma reactor, it is possible to define material proper es, boundary condi ons, and applied poten als by using the Electrosta c Module. Also, the Laminar Flow Module specifies fluid proper es, inlet/outlet condi ons, and flow behavior within the reactor.

div.head: E. Plasma chemistry (principles and mechanisms of plasma for water interac on)
div.p: More than a dozen studies focused on the discovery or suggested ac ve par cles and their mechanisms of ac on. A total of 79 gas phase species, 83 water phase species, 1680 gas phase reac ons and 448 plasma reac ons are included in the complete reac on mechanism 37 , which can be summarized as follows:

div.head: Chemical proper es of gas-phase plasma interac on with water
div.p: Plasma reacts to water, triggering a range of kine c chemical reac ons and giving rise to the existence of an Aqueous reac ve species. Different processes, e.g. the transfer of gases to liquids and a chemical reac on between gas types and liquid molecules are performed when PAW is generated at an interface layer 38 . The interac on of water with the plasma results in non-equilibrium dissocia on of water molecules, leading to the forma on of forma on of shortliving species such as hydroxyl ions (OH -) and hydrated (solvated) electrons. A more stable species, including super oxides, ozone, H2O2, is formed by subsequent rapid reac ons between hydroxyl ions and hydrated(solvated) electrons 39 . Apart from ROS, PAW also contain nitric (HNO3) and nitrous acid (HNO2) and low-level transient RNS, e.g. peroxynitrous acid/peroxynitrite and nitrogen dioxide radicals. Nitrogen oxide, which then reacts with water to form acids, is formed in the presence of air when nitrogen and oxygen are separated from the gaseous phase. The reac ons are as follows 37,40 :
NO2 + OH → HNO3 (18)
H2O2 + H + + NO2 -→ ONOOH + H2O (20)
NO + OH • → HNO2
HNO2 + OH • → NO2 + H2O
OH + NO2 → [O=N-OOH] → O=N-OO -+ H + (26)

div.head: Chemical proper es of gas-phase plasma interac on with HRW
div.p: With the presence of hydrogen in HRW, other reac ons can also occur. The formula of these reac ons is given below 37,37,40 .
e -+H2→ e -+ H2 ; where N is the number of seeds germinated every day and T is the number of days from seeding corresponding to N.

div.head: Germina on rate index (GRI)
div.p: 𝐺𝑅𝐼 = + + ⋯ + ; where G1 is the germina on percentage on day 1, G2 is the germina on parentage at day 2; and so on.

div.head: III. Results and Discussion
div.p: A. Simula ons FIG. 4 shows the distribu on of the electric poten al and the field lines for the electric field. Addi onally, in Figure 5 we can see the 2D lines of electric field for direct and indirect arc reactors and the differences between them as well.

The intensity of the electric field must be at least 10 MV/m in order for to an electric discharge occurs at atmospheric pressure. A comparison is shown in FIG. 5 of the intensity of the electric field generated by two reactor geometries; it is shown that in both of these geometries, an electric discharge can be generated to the required limit value. In order to inves gate the effects of electric discharge on reactor behavior and fluid dynamics, the arc discharge was modeled by applying a space charge, which is shown in FIG. 6. As shown in FIG. 6.a, the arc discharge pa ern is unstable as a result of fluid flow. In contrast, in direct arc discharge as illustrated in FIG. 6.b, it is symmetrical and intense.

Despite the fact that the direct discharge reactor does not have an inlet gas as can be seen in FIG. 7, the movement of the charge par cles within the arc ini ates a fluid flow both within and around the arc. As a result of fluid flow, ac ve species produced in plasma are transferred to water, then the fluid speed at the water surface is an important parameter. Also due to the movement of fluid within the electric arc area, water enters the discharge area, leading to the forma on of ac ve hydrogen species.

div.head: B. Op cal Emission Spectra
div.p: FIG. 8 illustrates the op cal emission spectra of GAD and Glow discharge electrolysis at wavelengths ranging from 100 to 200 nm. An op cal emission spectrometer, Technooran (Noora200), was used to iden fy the reac ve species present in the generated discharge.

At wavelengths ranging from 200 nm to 270 nm, the low-intensity excited nitric oxide (NOγ-band) dominates the spectra 42 . The existence of the hydroxyl radical (OH) is indicated by the signal at 309 nm. At wavelengths ranging from 315 nm to 380 nm, several high and low-intensity nitrogen molecules (N2_the second positive system) were also discovered 43 . In the spectrum of FIG. 8. Spectrum of plasmas formed by a) electrical discharge on the water surface and b) arc discharge in air arc in water, due to the presence of many reactive oxygen species, the intensity of excited nitric oxide species and hydroxyl radical is the highest. Low-intensity (N2 -the initial negative system) is also observed at wavelengths ranging from 390 to 430 nm 44 . Oxygen (O2) is also present in the discharge, as shown by the emission spectrum's peak at 356 nm 45 . All of these reactive species are short-lived and produce reactive species that are long-lived 8,40,43,46 . These excited N2 molecules react with O2 in the air and turn it into NOx. The dissolution of produced NOx in water leads to the formation of stable active nitrogen species (RNS) such as NO2¯ and NO3¯ ions (nitrogen and nitric acid). When water comes into direct contact with plasma, it heats up and vaporizes, causing the formation of plasma through the interaction of air and water molecules. This process produces more oxygenated and nitrogenated species, which can be seen in the plasma spectrum.

div.head: C. Physicochemical Proper es of water
div.p: The pH, electrical conduc vity and total dissolved solids of the ac vated water were measured to determine their physicochemical proper es. The concentra on of hydrogen ions in the solu on is measured by the pH. Table I shows a decrease in pH levels in PAWs, but injec ng hydrogen gas leads to a slight increase in pH. The pH changes in PAWs acidifica on are consistent with other ar cles 9,12-14 . Hopkins' paper indicates that pH levels should rise in HRW 47 .
The reac on of water with the species produced in the plasma causes PAW to become acidic. The PAW shall become more acid as the quan ty of ROS and RNS delivered to the water increases.
Changes in ionic species within PAWs were first iden fied by measuring their electrical conduc vity. The increases in conduc vity are due to the forma on of ROS and RNS, when ac ve water is prepared.

Hydrogens are the lightest gases and they react with most elements under normal condi ons, whereas H2 is not very reac ve in its molecular state 48 . At the interface of plasma with water, hydrogen molecules are also decomposed in sample HPAW2. More reac ons occur, and hydrogen radicals produce more of the secondary compounds. There is a growing possibility that more compounds will be dissolved in the water.

div.head: D. Water Raman spectra
div.p: The Raman spectra of the water samples were collected using a Technooran (Microspectrophotometer, Ram-532-004) in the range of 134-4325 cm -1 with an exposure me of 500 ms and three accumula ons. Ac vated water's Raman spectrum is illustrated in FIG. 9. Using the reference data available in the literature, the Raman spectrum of water can be seen in FIG.9 lines (A) and (B), with vibra on bands near 3400 and 3250 cm -1 49 . The Raman Q Table I Physicochemical proper es of ac vated waters branch of hydrogen dissolved in water is observed at wavelengths ranging from 3900 cm - 1 to 4170 cm -150 . In FIG. 9., line (c) represents a peak of the Q-branch.

Configura on of two hydrogen bonds and two covalent bonds between oxygen and hydrogens around oxygen atom is consistent with the tetrahedral-like structure of five water molecules 51 . As the impuri es increase in the water, the four-hole configura on is prevented from forming, and the peak height is reduced. Alterna vely, hydrogen molecules can contribute to this structure's forma on and intensify its peak.

In addi on to HRW, HPAW1 and HPAW2, hydrogen peak can also be observed in PAW1 and PAW2 due to reac between plasma ac ve species and liquids. Water peak intensity has decreased in PAW2 compared to PAW1 and HPAW2 compared to HPAW1 due to the increasing number of substances soluble in water. By forming a symmetrical configura on, hydrogen molecules in HPAW1/ HPAW2 have intensified the Raman Q-branch peak of water compared to PAW1/ PAW2.

div.head: FIG. 9. The Raman spectra of ac vated waters
E. Length and germina on parameters
div.p: The measurement results of 125 len l bean sprouts in each group were taken for length and germina on parameters in five replicates on the third day (FIG. 10). FIG. 11 unequivocally displays the obtained results.

The acronym FGP represents the final percentage of germina on, which serves as an indicator of the seed popula on's viability. The seeds from HPAW1 and HPAW2 had the highest FGP, with a reported percentage of 93.95%.

MGT, or Mean Germina on Time, refers to the average me required for seeds to germinate, taking into account the ming of the majority of germina on events. The lower the MGT, the faster a popula on of seeds has germinated 52 . The Control had the highest MGT, followed by HRW with a 10.5% decrease. PAW1 and HPAW1 both decreased by 13% and 12.7%, respec vely. The lowest MGT value belonged to PAW2 and HPAW2 with a d ecrease of around 12.8% compared to the reference sample. The CVG (Coefficient of velocity of germina on) solely measures the speed of germina on based on the quan ty of seeds and me, without placing emphasis on the final percentage 41 . It priori zes the me taken to achieve the result and does not consider specific me details, only general averages 53 . In case of PAW2/HPAW2 and PAW1/HPAW1 samples, nearly iden cal CVG is reported. In comparison to the control sample, almost iden cal CVG ra os were reported for 1.2 and 3.4 samples with increases of 18.4% and 14.7% respec vely. Compared to a reference the CVG increased by 12.5% for sample HRW. PAW's RONS can enhance germina on rates by ac va ng the abscisic acid/gibberellic acid pathways, interrup ng seed dormancy.

In the Germina on Index (GI), seeds germinated on the first day are given more weight than those germinated later. The GI measures both the percentage and speed of germina on, with a higher value indica ng a higher percentage and rate 54 . The highest recorded value for GI is related to HPAW2, with a 42.9% increase compared to the reference. With an increase of 38.9%, it is followed by PAW2 and HPAW1. HRW and PAW1 both increased by 36.4 and 24.8 respec vely. The GI is considered the most comprehensive measurement parameter, combining germina on percentage, speed, spread, dura on, and high/low events 41 .

There is a progressive differen a on of organs and ssue in developing legume seeds. The matura on is governed by a signaling network composed of sugars, amino acids, and SnRK1 kinases. Changing levels of oxygen energy and the nutrient state ac vates matura on processes. Embryo cells become green and photosynthe cally opera ve during the transi on. Sucrose func ons as a transport and nutrient sugar and as a signal molecule that ac vates storage-associated processes 55 . HRW plays a vital role in the conversion of starch to sugars 56 , which are simple carbohydrates made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen 57 . The accumula on of soluble proteins not only aids water reten on but also protects essen al substances. This leads to maximizing plant growth and promo ng good health 58 .

div.head: IV. Conclusion
div.p: In this study, different PAW reactors and hydrogen injec on systems were used to produce ac ve species in water. HRW-irrigated seeds have be er parameters than control seeds, as evident from the results. The presence of RONS groups in PAW water s mulates seed metabolism and accelerates growth. The amount of compounds dissolved in water changes depending on the type of plasma system used. HPAW and PAW irriga on may result in a significantly higher yield in a shorter period of me, based on our findings. Future studies assessing the long-term effects of plasma and hydrogen treatment should examine the biological and chemical changes in the sprouts and their natural microbiota.

figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. Schema c of the water ac va ng setups; a) PAW1 (injec on of electrically arc discharged air into water); b) PAW2 (arc discharge on the water surface); and c) HRW generator

Chemical proper es of gas-phase plasma without waterChemical reac ons taking place during discharge in atmospheric air (its primary components, typically nitrogen and oxygen) resul ng in the forma on of ROS and RNS.O2 + e → O + + O + 2e (

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. The schema c geometry of two indirect arc reactors (a) and a direct arc reactor(b).

Germina on index (GI): 𝐺𝐼 = (3 × 𝑁1) + (2 × 𝑁2) + (1 × 𝑁3) ; where N1, N2 and N3 is the number of germinated seeds on the first, second and third day and the mul pliers (e.g., 1, 2, and 3) are weights given to the days of the germina on.

figure.head: FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. 2D surface of poten al and electric field lines for indirect arc reactor (a) and direct arc reactor (b).

figure.head: FIG. 6 .

figure.head: FIG. 10 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 10. Len l seeds treatment using PAW, HRW and control on the third day

div.head: Data Availability
div.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
